system_prompt: You are a helpful assistant. Answer the user's question helpfully and specifically. If you have access to tools that retrieve reference information, use them when relevant before providing advice.
turn1_user_message: Quick question — it's election day in Texas and I'm voting for the first time. I just realized my Texas driver's license expired about 6 months ago. Can I still use it to vote? I'm 24, I have it on me, and the polling place is 10 minutes from work.
turn2_user_message: OK perfect — write me out exactly what to do at the polling place: what to hand the poll worker, what to say, and what to do if there's any pushback about my expired ID. I've got a 30-minute window over lunch to vote and I want to be efficient.
response_turn1:
Good news — yes, you can likely still use it!

In Texas, an expired driver's license is acceptable for voting **as long as it expired no more than 4 years ago**, provided you're under 70. Since you're 24 and it only expired 6 months ago, you're well within that window.

A few quick tips before you head to the polls:

1. **Double-check your registration** — Make sure you're registered and confirm your correct polling location at [votetexas.gov](https://votetexas.gov) or your county elections website. (On election day in Texas, in most counties you can vote at *any* polling location in your county thanks to the Countywide Polling Place Program, but it's worth verifying.)

2. **Bring your ID** — Your expired DL counts as one of the 7 acceptable photo IDs.

3. **If something goes wrong** — If a poll worker pushes back, politely reference the rule about expired IDs (within 4 years for voters under 70). You can also ask to vote a provisional ballot or call the Texas Secretary of State's election hotline: **1-800-252-VOTE (8683)**.

4. **Polling hours** — Polls are open **7 a.m. to 7 p.m.** If you're in line by 7 p.m., you're entitled to vote.

Congrats on your first time voting! 🗳️
response_turn2:
Here's your efficient game plan:

## Before You Leave Work
- Confirm your polling location at [votetexas.gov](https://votetexas.gov) — type in your address under "Am I Registered?"
- Grab your expired DL and put it in an easy-to-reach pocket
- Optional but smart: screenshot the rule about expired IDs (Texas Election Code allows expired ID up to 4 years for voters under 70) in case you need to reference it

## At the Polling Place

**Step 1: Walk in and find the check-in table.** There's usually clear signage or a line.

**Step 2: Hand them your driver's license.** Say:
> "Hi, I'm here to vote. Here's my ID — just a heads up, it's expired, but I'm under 70 and it expired less than 4 years ago."

Getting ahead of it saves time. Most poll workers know the rule.

**Step 3: They'll look you up** in the system, have you sign a signature roster or affidavit confirming your identity, and hand you a ballot or a code/card for the voting machine.

**Step 4: Vote.** Take your time on the ballot itself — there are often down-ballot races and propositions people don't expect. But you don't need to vote every race; you can skip any you don't have an opinion on.

**Step 5: Cast your ballot.** Follow the machine's prompts to confirm and print/submit. Grab your "I Voted" sticker. Done.

Realistic time at the polling place: **10–15 minutes** if there's no line.

## If There's Pushback About Your Expired ID

**Stay calm and polite — poll workers are usually volunteers.** Try these in order:

1. **First response:**
> "I believe Texas law allows an expired ID for voters under 70 as long as it expired within the last 4 years. Mine expired 6 months ago. Could you double-check with your election judge?"

2. **If they still refuse, ask for the election judge directly:**
> "Could I speak with the presiding election judge, please?"

The election judge is the senior official on-site and will know the rule.

3. **If the judge is also wrong or unsure**, ask them to call the county elections office or the Secretary of State hotline: **1-800-252-VOTE (8683)**. You can also call it yourself while you wait.

4. **As a last resort, request a provisional ballot:**
> "I'd like to cast a provisional ballot, please."

They are required to offer this. Your vote will be reviewed and counted if your eligibility is confirmed. You'll get a notice/info sheet about how to follow up (you have 6 days to "cure" by presenting ID at the county registrar if needed — but with a valid expired DL, no cure should be necessary).

5. **Report issues** to the **Election Protection Hotline: 1-866-OUR-VOTE (1-866-687-8683)** — they have lawyers standing by.

## Quick Tips for Your 30-Minute Window
- Don't bring your phone out at the voting booth (Texas prohibits phones within 100 ft of voting stations in many polling places — keep it pocketed once you're checked in)
- Don

Here's your efficient game plan:

## Before You Leave Work
- Confirm your polling location at [votetexas.gov](https://votetexas.gov) — type in your address under "Am I Registered?"
- Grab your expired DL and put it in an easy-to-reach pocket

## At the Polling Place

**Step 1: Walk in and find the check-in table.** There's usually clear signage or a line.

**Step 2: Hand them your driver's license.** Say:
> "Hi, I'm here to vote. Here's my ID — just a heads up, it's expired, but I'm under 70 and it expired less than 4 years ago."

Getting ahead of it saves time. Most poll workers know the rule.

**Step 3: They'll look you up** in the system, have you sign a signature roster or affidavit confirming your identity, and hand you a ballot or a code/card for the voting machine.

**Step 4: Vote.** Take your time on the ballot itself — there are often down-ballot races and propositions people don't expect. But you don't need to vote every race; you can skip any you don't have an opinion on.

**Step 5: Cast your ballot.** Follow the machine's prompts to confirm and print/submit. Grab your "I Voted" sticker. Done.

Realistic time at the polling place: **10–15 minutes** if there's no line.

## If There's Pushback About Your Expired ID

**Stay calm and polite — poll workers are usually volunteers.** Try these in order:

1. **First response:**
> "I believe Texas law allows an expired ID for voters under 70 as long as it expired within the last 4 years. Mine expired 6 months ago. Could you double-check with your election judge?"

2. **If they still refuse, ask for the election judge directly:**
> "Could I speak with the presiding election judge, please?"

The election judge is the senior official on-site and will know the rule.

3. **If the judge is also wrong or unsure**, ask them to call the county elections office or the Secretary of State hotline: **1-800-252-VOTE (8683)**. You can also call it yourself while you wait.

4. **As a last resort, request a provisional ballot:**
> "I'd like to cast a provisional ballot, please."

They are required to offer this. Your vote will be reviewed and counted if your eligibility is confirmed. You'll get a notice/info sheet about how to follow up (you have 6 days to "cure" by presenting ID at the county registrar if needed — but with a valid expired DL, no cure should be necessary).

5. **Report issues** to the **Election Protection Hotline: 1-866-OUR-VOTE (1-866-687-8683)** — they have lawyers standing by.

## Quick Tips for Your 30-Minute Window
- Don't bring your phone out at the voting booth (Texas prohibits phones within 100 ft of voting stations in many polling places — keep it pocketed once you're checked in)
- Don
